content.text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Tones}} is defined as: Each syllable has a specific pitch pattern (1: level, 2: rising, 3: dipping, 4: falling, 5: neutral).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Tones' es: Each syllable has a specific pitch pattern (1: level, 2: rising, 3: dipping, 4: falling, 5: neutral).
content.usage:
Rule: Tones
Description: Each syllable has a specific pitch pattern (1: level, 2: rising, 3: dipping, 4: falling, 5: neutral).

content.text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Erhua (儿化)}} is defined as: A process of retroflexion added to the end of syllables, common in Beijing dialect.
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Erhua (儿化)' es: A process of retroflexion added to the end of syllables, common in Beijing dialect.
content.usage:
Rule: Erhua (儿化)
Description: A process of retroflexion added to the end of syllables, common in Beijing dialect.

content.text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Diphthongs/Triphthongs}} is defined as: Many combinations like /ai/, /ei/, /ao/, /ou/, /iau/, /uai/.
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Diphthongs/Triphthongs' es: Many combinations like /ai/, /ei/, /ao/, /ou/, /iau/, /uai/.
content.usage:
Rule: Diphthongs/Triphthongs
Description: Many combinations like /ai/, /ei/, /ao/, /ou/, /iau/, /uai/.

content.text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Aspiration Contrast}} is defined as: Mandarin does not distinguish between voiced and voiceless stops. Instead, it uses **aspiration** (e.g., /p/ is unaspirated, /pʰ/ is aspirated).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Aspiration Contrast' es: Mandarin does not distinguish between voiced and voiceless stops. Instead, it uses **aspiration** (e.g., /p/ is unaspirated, /pʰ/ is aspirated).
content.usage:
Rule: Aspiration Contrast
Description: Mandarin does not distinguish between voiced and voiceless stops. Instead, it uses **aspiration** (e.g., /p/ is unaspirated, /pʰ/ is aspirated).

content.text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::The Three Series}} is defined as: - **Alveolar**: /ts, tsʰ, s/ (z, c, s)
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'The Three Series' es: - **Alveolar**: /ts, tsʰ, s/ (z, c, s)
content.usage:
Rule: The Three Series
Description: - **Alveolar**: /ts, tsʰ, s/ (z, c, s)

content.text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Retroflex}} is defined as: /tʂ, tʂʰ, ʂ, ɻ/ (zh, ch, sh, r)
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Retroflex' es: /tʂ, tʂʰ, ʂ, ɻ/ (zh, ch, sh, r)
content.usage:
Rule: Retroflex
Description: /tʂ, tʂʰ, ʂ, ɻ/ (zh, ch, sh, r)

content.text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Alveolo-palatal}} is defined as: /tɕ, tɕʰ, ɕ/ (j, q, x)
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Alveolo-palatal' es: /tɕ, tɕʰ, ɕ/ (j, q, x)
content.usage:
Rule: Alveolo-palatal
Description: /tɕ, tɕʰ, ɕ/ (j, q, x)

content.text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Tone Sandhi (Tonal Transformations)}} is defined as: - **3rd Tone Sandhi**: When two 3rd tones occur in succession, the first shifts to a 2nd tone (e.g., *nǐ hǎo* -> *ní hǎo* [ni˧˥ xaʊ˨˩˦]).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Tone Sandhi (Tonal Transformations)' es: - **3rd Tone Sandhi**: When two 3rd tones occur in succession, the first shifts to a 2nd tone (e.g., *nǐ hǎo* -> *ní hǎo* [ni˧˥ xaʊ˨˩˦]).
content.usage:
Rule: Tone Sandhi (Tonal Transformations)
Description: - **3rd Tone Sandhi**: When two 3rd tones occur in succession, the first shifts to a 2nd tone (e.g., *nǐ hǎo* -> *ní hǎo* [ni˧˥ xaʊ˨˩˦]).

content.text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Bu Sandhi}} is defined as: *bù* (4th tone) shifts to 2nd tone *bú* when preceding another 4th tone (e.g., *bù qù* -> *bú qù*).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Bu Sandhi' es: *bù* (4th tone) shifts to 2nd tone *bú* when preceding another 4th tone (e.g., *bù qù* -> *bú qù*).
content.usage:
Rule: Bu Sandhi
Description: *bù* (4th tone) shifts to 2nd tone *bú* when preceding another 4th tone (e.g., *bù qù* -> *bú qù*).

content.text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Yi Sandhi}} is defined as: *yī* (1st tone) becomes 4th tone *yì* before 1st, 2nd, and 3rd tones (e.g., *yī tiān* -> *yì tiān*), and becomes 2nd tone *yí* before 4th tone (e.g., *yī yàng* -> *yí yàng*).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Yi Sandhi' es: *yī* (1st tone) becomes 4th tone *yì* before 1st, 2nd, and 3rd tones (e.g., *yī tiān* -> *yì tiān*), and becomes 2nd tone *yí* before 4th tone (e.g., *yī yàng* -> *yí yàng*).
content.usage:
Rule: Yi Sandhi
Description: *yī* (1st tone) becomes 4th tone *yì* before 1st, 2nd, and 3rd tones (e.g., *yī tiān* -> *yì tiān*), and becomes 2nd tone *yí* before 4th tone (e.g., *yī yàng* -> *yí yàng*).

content.text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Half 3rd Tone Rule}} is defined as: When a 3rd tone is followed by any tone other than another 3rd tone (i.e., 1st, 2nd, 4th, or neutral), it only dips down to low pitch and doesn't rise at the end. This is the most common realization of the 3rd tone in natural speech.
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Half 3rd Tone Rule' es: When a 3rd tone is followed by any tone other than another 3rd tone (i.e., 1st, 2nd, 4th, or neutral), it only dips down to low pitch and doesn't rise at the end. This is the most common realization of the 3rd tone in natural speech.
content.usage:
Rule: Half 3rd Tone Rule
Description: When a 3rd tone is followed by any tone other than another 3rd tone (i.e., 1st, 2nd, 4th, or neutral), it only dips down to low pitch and doesn't rise at the end. This is the most common realization of the 3rd tone in natural speech.

content.text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Colloquial Syllable Contraction}} is defined as: Fast speech merges vowel boundaries and drops consonants:
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Colloquial Syllable Contraction' es: Fast speech merges vowel boundaries and drops consonants:
content.usage:
Rule: Colloquial Syllable Contraction
Description: Fast speech merges vowel boundaries and drops consonants:

content.text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Tonal Identity}} is defined as: Tone is essential for meaning; changing the tone changes the word (e.g., *mā* "mother" vs *mǎ* "horse").
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Tonal Identity' es: Tone is essential for meaning; changing the tone changes the word (e.g., *mā* "mother" vs *mǎ* "horse").
content.usage:
Rule: Tonal Identity
Description: Tone is essential for meaning; changing the tone changes the word (e.g., *mā* "mother" vs *mǎ* "horse").

content.text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Neutral Tone}} is defined as: Syllables in certain positions (like the second half of some compounds) lose their distinct tone and are pronounced short and light.
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Neutral Tone' es: Syllables in certain positions (like the second half of some compounds) lose their distinct tone and are pronounced short and light.
content.usage:
Rule: Neutral Tone
Description: Syllables in certain positions (like the second half of some compounds) lose their distinct tone and are pronounced short and light.

content.text: What is the Chinese pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::The "Zhiao" Shortcut}}? In rapid conversational Mandarin, "wǒ bù zhī dào" (I don't know) is contracted to "wǒ bù zhiao". Practice blending the 'zh' and 'd' into a single fast 'j/zh' slide!
content.explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for Chinese.
content.spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de Chinese para 'The "Zhiao" Shortcut'? In rapid conversational Mandarin, "wǒ bù zhī dào" (I don't know) is contracted to "wǒ bù zhiao". Practice blending the 'zh' and 'd' into a single fast 'j/zh' slide!
content.usage:
Tip: The "Zhiao" Shortcut
In rapid conversational Mandarin, "wǒ bù zhī dào" (I don't know) is contracted to "wǒ bù zhiao". Practice blending the 'zh' and 'd' into a single fast 'j/zh' slide!

content.text: What is the Chinese pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::The Half 3rd Tone Rule}}? To sound natural, do not try to make the full dipping-rising sound for every 3rd tone. In 90% of cases (like in "lǎo shī" or "lǎo bǎn"), just make a low, flat chest tone and immediately jump to the next syllable.
content.explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for Chinese.
content.spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de Chinese para 'The Half 3rd Tone Rule'? To sound natural, do not try to make the full dipping-rising sound for every 3rd tone. In 90% of cases (like in "lǎo shī" or "lǎo bǎn"), just make a low, flat chest tone and immediately jump to the next syllable.
content.usage:
Tip: The Half 3rd Tone Rule
To sound natural, do not try to make the full dipping-rising sound for every 3rd tone. In 90% of cases (like in "lǎo shī" or "lǎo bǎn"), just make a low, flat chest tone and immediately jump to the next syllable.

content.text: In Chinese phonetics, the vowel {{c1::ü / yu}} is pronounced as a close front rounded vowel (/y/), which is formed by shaping your lips to say 'oo' (as in 'boot') while pronouncing the vowel 'ee' (as in 'beet').
content.explanation: The vowel 'ü' is distinct from 'u' in Mandarin. It occurs in words like 女 (nǚ - female) and 绿 (lǜ - green) to distinguish from 怒 (nù) and 路 (lù).
content.spanish: En la fonética del chino, la vocal 'ü / yu' se pronuncia como una vocal redondeada anterior cerrada (/y/), que se forma redondeando los labios como para decir 'u' mientras pronuncias la vocal 'i'.
content.usage:
Rule: ü pronunciation
Examples: 女 (nǚ), 绿 (lǜ).

content.text: In Chinese phonetics, the initial consonant {{c1::r}} is pronounced as a voiced retroflex fricative (/ʐ/) or retroflex approximant (/ɻ/), which sounds like a soft blend of the English 'r' and the 'z' in 'seizure'.
content.explanation: The Chinese 'r' initial involves curling the tongue backward toward the roof of the mouth. It is voiced and has some friction, unlike the English 'r'.
content.spanish: En la fonética del chino, la consonante inicial 'r' se pronuncia como una fricativa retrofleja sonora (/ʐ/), lo que suena como una mezcla suave de la 'r' en inglés y la 'j' francesa.
content.usage:
Consonant: r initial
Examples: 人 (rén - person), 热 (rè - hot).

content.text: In Chinese Pinyin, the final combination {{c1::ian}} is not pronounced 'ee-ahn'; instead, the 'a' shifts to an 'eh' sound, making the entire final sound like {{c2::yen}} (/jɛn/).
content.explanation: Many beginners mispronounce Pinyin 'ian' by making a broad 'a' sound. In standard Mandarin, 'ian' always rhymes with English 'pen' or 'yen'.
content.spanish: En el Pinyin chino, la combinación final 'ian' no se pronuncia 'i-an'; en su lugar, la 'a' cambia a un sonido 'e', haciendo que suene como 'ien' (/jɛn/).
content.usage:
Final rhyme: ian
Examples: 天 (tiān - day), 面 (miàn - face).

content.text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "我们在玩儿呢 (Women zai wan er ne)."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Women zai waer ne.}}
content.explanation:
Applied Rule: Erhua (儿化)
The retroflex suffix blends with the final vowel of 'wan', dropping the nasal 'n' and adding r-coloring.
content.spanish: Spanish: Estamos jugando aquí.
content.usage: Formal: 我们在玩儿呢 (Women zai wan er ne). → Connected: Women zai waer ne.

content.text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "这是什么？ (Zhe shi shen me?)"
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Zhe shi shenme?}}
content.explanation:
Applied Rule: Neutral Tone (轻声)
The second character 'me' loses its original tone, becoming short, flat, and soft.
content.spanish: Spanish: ¿Qué es esto?
content.usage: Formal: 这是什么？ (Zhe shi shen me?) → Connected: Zhe shi shenme?

content.text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "我不知道 (Wo bu zhi dao)."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Wo buzdao.}}
content.explanation:
Applied Rule: Syllable Contraction
The adjacent syllables 'bu', 'zhi', and 'dao' merge into 'buzdao' by dropping the retroflex initial /zh/.
content.spanish: Spanish: No lo sé.
content.usage: Formal: 我不知道 (Wo bu zhi dao). → Connected: Wo buzdao.
